ar.register: Modern conversational MSA, natural, not literary
rules: Use active voice, not passive. Say 'نحلل موقعك' (we analyze your site) not 'يتم تحليل الموقع' (the site is being analyzed).
Use personal pronouns and possessive suffixes: 'جلستك' (your session), 'موقعك' (your site). Never use 'الجلسة الخاصة بك'.
For ongoing actions, use 'جارٍ + verbal noun' pattern: 'جارٍ التحميل...' (Loading...). Keep it short.
Avoid formal particles like 'إنّ', 'لقد', 'حيث'. They make UI text feel like a newspaper.
Keep button labels and short strings punchy: 1-3 words max.
Numbers stay in Western Arabic numerals (0-9), not Eastern (٠-٩).
Use masculine default for generic UI text (Arabic convention for addressing unknown users).

When mixing Arabic and Latin script (brand names, tech terms like URL, Enter), place the Latin word BEFORE the Arabic text so RTL reading flows naturally right-to-left. Example: 'Runwell مدعوم من' not 'مدعوم من Runwell'. The Latin word sits at the visual left (end of RTL line).
Never use em dashes or en dashes. Rewrite with natural punctuation for the target language.

fr.register: Formal 'vous', professional but approachable tech product
rules: Use 'vous' not 'tu'. Formal register by default.

ALL French text MUST include proper diacritical marks (accents). NEVER output unaccented French. Required characters: é, è, ê, ë, à, â, ù, û, ô, î, ï, ç. Example: 'opportunité' not 'opportunite', 'stratégique' not 'strategique', 'évalué' not 'evalue'.
Contractions are OK: 'l'analyse' not 'la analyse'.
Keep button labels short and direct.
Use present tense for actions: 'Analyser' not 'Veuillez analyser'.
Preserve English tech terms where natural: 'chat', 'URL', 'screenshot' if commonly used.
Never use em dashes or en dashes. Rewrite with natural punctuation for the target language.
Use 'Sie' form consistently.
Compound nouns are fine; don't avoid them artificially.
Keep button labels concise: prefer infinitive form ('Starten' not 'Klicken Sie hier zum Starten').
Use accepted anglicisms where natural: 'Website', 'Chat', 'Screenshot'.
Numbers and punctuation follow German conventions.
Never use em dashes or en dashes. Rewrite with natural punctuation for the target language.
Use 'tú' not 'vos' or 'usted'. Neutral Latin American Spanish.
Avoid Spain-specific vocabulary (e.g., 'ordenador' → use 'computadora' or just 'sitio web').
Keep button labels direct: imperative form ('Iniciar', 'Enviar').
Preserve English tech terms where commonly used: 'chat', 'URL'.
Use inclusive language where possible.
Never use em dashes or en dashes. Rewrite with natural punctuation for the target language.

tn.register: Tunisian Darija for chat/conversational, MSA for formal UI pages. Warm, local, approachable
rules: Use Tunisian vocabulary where natural: 'مرحبا بيك' (welcome), 'كيفاش' (how), 'وقتاش' (when), 'فين' (where).
Keep UI labels in simple MSA that Tunisians understand: 'الصفحة الرئيسية' (Home), 'اتصل بنا' (Contact).
For chat/bot context, use Darija tone: 'أهلا! كيفاش نعاونك؟' not 'مرحباً! كيف يمكنني مساعدتك؟'.
Numbers stay in Western Arabic numerals (0-9), not Eastern (٠-٩).
Use masculine default for generic UI text (Arabic convention).
French loanwords are OK where Tunisians naturally use them: 'réservation', 'menu', 'parking'.
Keep button labels punchy: 1-3 words max.
Avoid literary MSA particles ('إنّ', 'لقد', 'حيث'). Tunisians find them distant.

When mixing Arabic and Latin script (brand names, tech terms like URL, Enter), place the Latin word BEFORE the Arabic text so RTL reading flows naturally right-to-left. Example: 'Runwell يعمل بتقنية' not 'يعمل بتقنية Runwell'. The Latin word sits at the visual left (end of RTL line).
Never use em dashes or en dashes. Rewrite with natural punctuation for the target language.
